title: How to Roll Back a Kubernetes Deployment
content:
This runbook describes the steps required to safely roll back a Kubernetes deployment when a release introduces a regression or service degradation.

Prerequisites: You must have kubectl configured with the correct cluster context and sufficient RBAC permissions to manage deployments in the target namespace.

Step 1 — Identify the affected deployment. Run 'kubectl get deployments -n <namespace>' to list all deployments. Confirm the name and namespace of the deployment you need to roll back.

Step 2 — Check rollout history. Run 'kubectl rollout history deployment/<name> -n <namespace>' to see the revision history. Each revision corresponds to a previous deployment state. Note the revision number you want to restore.

Step 3 — Perform the rollback. To roll back to the immediately previous revision, run 'kubectl rollout undo deployment/<name> -n <namespace>'. To roll back to a specific revision, append '--to-revision=<number>'. Kubernetes will immediately begin replacing running pods with the previous ReplicaSet.

Step 4 — Monitor the rollout. Run 'kubectl rollout status deployment/<name> -n <namespace>' to watch the rollout progress. Wait until the output confirms 'successfully rolled out' before declaring the incident resolved.

Step 5 — Verify application health. Check your monitoring dashboards and confirm that error rates, latency, and pod restart counts have returned to baseline. If the service exposes a health endpoint, curl it directly to confirm a 200 response.

Step 6 — Document the incident. Open a post-mortem ticket and record the revision rolled back from, the revision restored to, the time of rollback, and the on-call engineer who performed it. Link the ticket to the original deployment pull request.

Common pitfalls: Rolling back a deployment does not roll back changes to ConfigMaps, Secrets, or database migrations. If the regression involves a schema change, coordinate with the database team before rolling back the application. Also note that if the previous image has been deleted from the container registry, the rollback will fail with an ImagePullBackOff error — verify image availability in the registry before initiating the rollback.

title: Incident Response Runbook
content:
This runbook defines the incident response process for production outages and service degradations. All on-call engineers are expected to follow this process.

Severity Levels:
- SEV1: Complete service outage or data loss affecting all users. Requires immediate response, 24/7.
- SEV2: Significant degradation affecting a majority of users or a critical feature. Response within 15 minutes.
- SEV3: Partial degradation or elevated error rates affecting a minority of users. Response within 1 hour.
- SEV4: Minor issue with no user impact but requiring investigation. Response within 1 business day.

Step 1 — Acknowledge the alert. When PagerDuty fires, acknowledge the alert within 5 minutes for SEV1/SEV2. Failure to acknowledge escalates to the secondary on-call engineer automatically.

Step 2 — Open an incident channel. Create a Slack channel named #inc-<date>-<short-description> and post the PagerDuty link. Pin the channel to the #incidents Slack channel. All communication during the incident must happen in this channel.

Step 3 — Assess and assign severity. Based on the blast radius and user impact, assign the appropriate severity level. If unsure, default to the higher severity and downgrade once you have more information.

Step 4 — Identify the incident commander. For SEV1/SEV2, the on-call engineer is the incident commander unless they hand off explicitly. The incident commander coordinates communication, delegates investigation tasks, and provides status updates every 15 minutes.

Step 5 — Investigate and mitigate. Use your observability stack (Elasticsearch, Kibana dashboards, distributed traces) to identify the failure point. Prioritize mitigation over root cause analysis — restoring service comes first. Common mitigations include rolling back a deployment (see runbook-k8s-rollback), scaling up a service, or toggling a feature flag.

Step 6 — Communicate with stakeholders. For SEV1/SEV2, post a status update to the company status page within 10 minutes of acknowledgment. Update the status page every 30 minutes until resolution.

Step 7 — Resolve and close. Once service is restored, mark the incident resolved in PagerDuty. Post a final update to the incident channel and the status page. Archive the incident channel.

Step 8 — Post-mortem. All SEV1 and SEV2 incidents require a written post-mortem within 48 hours. Use the post-mortem template in Confluence. The post-mortem must include a timeline, root cause analysis, contributing factors, and a list of action items with owners and due dates.

title: Reindexing Elasticsearch Indices Without Downtime
content:
This runbook describes how to reindex an Elasticsearch index without taking the application offline. This is required when you need to change a mapping (for example, adding a new field type, changing an analyzer, or enabling document-level security).

Background: Elasticsearch does not support changing existing field mappings in place. If you need to change a field's type or add a field that requires reprocessing existing documents, you must create a new index with the updated mapping and migrate all documents to it.

Step 1 — Create the new index. Define the updated mapping and create the new index with a versioned name, for example 'knowledge-base-v2'. Do not use the alias name as the index name.

Step 2 — Set up an index alias. If you are not already using an alias, create one pointing to the current index: 'POST /_aliases { "actions": [{ "add": { "index": "knowledge-base-v1", "alias": "knowledge-base" }}]}'. Your application should always read and write through the alias, not the index name directly.

Step 3 — Start the reindex operation. Use the Reindex API: 'POST /_reindex { "source": { "index": "knowledge-base-v1" }, "dest": { "index": "knowledge-base-v2" }}'. For large indices, add '"conflicts": "proceed"' to skip version conflicts and run the reindex asynchronously by appending '?wait_for_completion=false'. Note the task ID returned.

Step 4 — Monitor reindex progress. Check progress with 'GET /_tasks/<task_id>'. The response includes the number of documents created, updated, and any failures.

Step 5 — Index documents written during reindex. Because your application continues writing to the old index during reindex, you need to run a second reindex scoped to documents updated after the reindex started. Use a range query on your 'last_updated' field to catch these.

Step 6 — Swap the alias atomically. Once the second reindex pass completes, swap the alias in a single atomic operation: 'POST /_aliases { "actions": [{ "remove": { "index": "knowledge-base-v1", "alias": "knowledge-base" }}, { "add": { "index": "knowledge-base-v2", "alias": "knowledge-base" }}]}'. The alias swap is atomic — there is no window where the alias points to nothing.

Step 7 — Verify and clean up. Confirm that your application is reading and writing correctly through the alias against the new index. Run your retrieval evaluation suite (see the evaluation tip in the knowledge base article) to confirm search quality is unchanged. Once satisfied, delete the old index to recover disk space.

title: Engineering Onboarding Guide
content:
Welcome to the engineering team. This guide covers everything you need to get set up in your first two weeks.

Week 1 — Access and tooling.

Day 1: Your manager will send calendar invites for onboarding sessions and introduce you to your onboarding buddy. Your first tasks are to set up your laptop using the IT setup guide (see guide-it-laptop-setup in the knowledge base), request access to the systems listed below, and join the mandatory Slack channels.

Required system access (submit requests via the IT portal):
- GitHub organization (engineering team)
- AWS console (read-only initially; write access after security training)
- PagerDuty (on-call schedule will be assigned after week 4)
- Datadog and Kibana (observability)
- Confluence and Jira (documentation and project tracking)
- 1Password (shared secrets vault)

Mandatory Slack channels: #engineering-general, #incidents, #deployments, #on-call-handoffs, #engineering-announcements.

Day 2–3: Complete the security training module in Workday. This is mandatory before you receive write access to any production system. The training takes approximately 3 hours and covers data handling policies, access control principles, and incident reporting.

Day 4–5: Set up your local development environment. Clone the main application repository and follow the README to get the stack running locally. If you hit issues, ask in #dev-environment — do not spend more than 30 minutes blocked without asking for help.

Week 2 — Codebase and process.

Spend the first half of the week reading through the architecture decision records (ADRs) in Confluence. Pay particular attention to the ADRs covering the service mesh, the event streaming architecture, and the data storage strategy — these come up frequently in design discussions.

By the end of week 2 you should have submitted at least one pull request, even if it is a documentation fix or a minor code cleanup. Getting your first PR through review helps you understand the team's review culture and CI/CD pipeline.

Your 30-60-90 day plan will be set with your manager at the end of week 1. Expect your first independent feature ownership to begin around day 45.

title: Code Review Guidelines
content:
This guide describes expectations for both authors and reviewers during the code review process.

For authors:

Keep pull requests small and focused. A PR that changes one thing is easier to review, easier to roll back, and easier to understand in git history six months later. As a rule of thumb, aim for under 400 lines of meaningful change. If a PR is larger, consider whether it can be split.

Write a clear description. The PR description should explain what the change does, why it is being made, and any context the reviewer needs that isn't obvious from the diff. Link to the Jira ticket. If there are alternative approaches you considered and rejected, mention them.

Self-review before requesting review. Read your own diff as if you were the reviewer. Catch typos, remove debug logging, and make sure tests cover the new behavior.

Respond to feedback constructively. If you disagree with a review comment, discuss it — don't silently ignore it or merge without resolving it. Unresolved discussions block merge.

For reviewers:

Review within one business day. Letting PRs sit in the review queue for more than a day slows the team down. If you are too busy, say so and suggest another reviewer.

Distinguish blocking from non-blocking comments. Prefix non-blocking suggestions with 'nit:' so the author knows it is optional. Reserve blocking comments for correctness issues, security concerns, and violations of team standards.

Review for correctness first, style second. Make sure the change does what it claims and doesn't introduce bugs or regressions before commenting on naming conventions or formatting. If there is a linter, let it handle style.

Approve when you are satisfied, not when you are tired. An approval is a signal that you are comfortable with the change shipping. If you have unresolved concerns, request changes rather than approving with caveats in a comment.

title: Parental Leave Policy
content:
This policy describes the company's parental leave entitlements for all full-time employees.

Eligibility: All full-time employees who have completed at least six months of continuous employment are eligible for parental leave. Part-time employees are eligible on a pro-rated basis.

Primary caregiver leave: Employees who are the primary caregiver following the birth, adoption, or fostering of a child are entitled to 20 weeks of fully paid leave. Leave may begin up to four weeks before the expected birth date or placement date.

Secondary caregiver leave: Employees who are the secondary caregiver are entitled to 6 weeks of fully paid leave, to be taken within the first year following the birth or placement of the child.

Additional unpaid leave: Following the paid leave period, employees may request up to 12 additional weeks of unpaid leave. Unpaid leave requests must be submitted in writing to HR at least four weeks in advance.

Benefits continuation: Health, dental, and vision benefits continue unchanged during paid parental leave. During unpaid leave, the employee is responsible for the employer's share of benefit premiums.

Return to work: Employees returning from parental leave are entitled to return to their previous role or a role of equivalent seniority, scope, and compensation. Employees are encouraged to discuss flexible working arrangements with their manager before returning.

How to apply: Submit a parental leave request through the HR portal at least eight weeks before the intended start date. Attach supporting documentation (birth certificate, adoption order, or fostering agreement) as soon as it is available. HR will confirm your leave dates and pay schedule within five business days.

title: Remote Work Policy
content:
This policy sets out the company's expectations and guidelines for employees working remotely.

Eligibility: Remote work is available to all full-time employees in roles that do not require a physical presence. Eligibility is determined by the employee's manager in consultation with HR. Employees in their first 90 days of employment are expected to work from the office at least three days per week.

Core hours: All employees, regardless of location, are expected to be available between 10:00 and 16:00 in their team's primary time zone. Outside core hours, employees may structure their workday as suits them, provided they meet their commitments and attend scheduled meetings.

Equipment and workspace: The company provides a laptop and peripherals for all employees. Remote employees are responsible for maintaining a suitable workspace with a stable internet connection of at least 25 Mbps download and 10 Mbps upload. The company offers a one-time home office stipend of $500 for employees who transition to fully remote work.

Security requirements: Remote employees must connect to company systems through the corporate VPN at all times. Use of public Wi-Fi without VPN is prohibited. Screens must not be visible to others when working with confidential information.

In-office expectations: Fully remote employees are expected to attend company all-hands events in person (typically quarterly) and any team on-sites scheduled by their manager. Travel costs for these events are covered by the company.

Review and revocation: Remote work arrangements are reviewed annually. The company reserves the right to require an employee to work from the office if business needs change or if performance issues arise that are attributed to the remote arrangement.

title: Paid Time Off Policy
content:
This policy describes the company's paid time off (PTO) entitlements and guidelines for requesting and using leave.

Entitlement: All full-time employees receive 25 days of PTO per calendar year, accruing at a rate of 2.08 days per month. New employees begin accruing PTO from their first day of employment.

Carryover: Employees may carry over up to 5 unused PTO days into the following calendar year. Days in excess of 5 are forfeited on December 31. Employees are encouraged to plan leave throughout the year to avoid forfeitures.

Requesting leave: PTO requests must be submitted through the HR portal. Requests of 3 days or fewer require at least 3 business days notice. Requests of 4 days or more require at least 2 weeks notice. Requests are subject to manager approval based on team capacity.

Blackout periods: Certain periods may be designated as blackout periods during which PTO requests will not be approved except in exceptional circumstances. Blackout periods are communicated at the start of each quarter and typically coincide with major product releases or fiscal year-end.

Sick leave: Sick leave is separate from PTO and is not subject to the carryover or accrual rules above. Employees receive 10 days of sick leave per year. Unused sick leave does not carry over. Sick leave can be used for personal illness, medical appointments, or caring for an immediate family member who is ill.

Termination: Upon termination of employment, accrued but unused PTO is paid out at the employee's current base salary rate. Sick leave is not paid out upon termination.

title: ADR-001: Adopt Kafka for Event Streaming
content:
Architecture Decision Record 001 — Adopt Apache Kafka as the company's event streaming platform.

Status: Accepted.

Context: As the platform has grown, several services have developed point-to-point integrations for exchanging data in real time. Each integration uses a different mechanism (REST webhooks, polling, shared database tables), resulting in brittle coupling, duplicated logic, and difficulty reasoning about data flow across the system. We need a unified, durable, and scalable mechanism for services to produce and consume events asynchronously.

Decision: We will adopt Apache Kafka (managed via Confluent Cloud) as the standard event streaming platform. All new inter-service integrations that involve asynchronous data exchange must use Kafka topics. Existing point-to-point integrations should be migrated to Kafka on a rolling basis, prioritized by fragility and team capacity.

Consequences:

Positive: Decouples producers and consumers. Enables replay and audit of event history. Provides a single observable plane for data flow. Scales horizontally without changes to producers or consumers.

Negative: Adds operational complexity for teams unfamiliar with Kafka. Introduces eventual consistency in flows that previously used synchronous REST. Requires new conventions around topic naming, schema management (we will use Confluent Schema Registry with Avro), and consumer group management.

Alternatives considered:
1. RabbitMQ — rejected because it lacks durable log replay and does not scale to the event volumes projected over the next 18 months.
2. AWS SQS/SNS — rejected because it ties us to a single cloud provider and lacks the schema management capabilities we require.
3. Continue with point-to-point REST — rejected because this is the root cause of the current fragility.

Owner: Platform Engineering team. Review date: June 2025.

title: ADR-002: Use Elasticsearch as the Primary Search and Analytics Store
content:
Architecture Decision Record 002 — Adopt Elasticsearch as the primary store for search, analytics, and AI-powered retrieval features.

Status: Accepted.

Context: The product requires full-text search, faceted filtering, real-time analytics dashboards, and increasingly AI-powered retrieval features such as semantic search and RAG (retrieval-augmented generation). We previously used a combination of PostgreSQL full-text search for document retrieval and a separate BI tool for analytics. This split creates data synchronization overhead and limits our ability to combine keyword search, semantic search, and analytics in a single query.

Decision: We will adopt Elasticsearch as the primary store for any feature involving search, analytics, or vector-based retrieval. PostgreSQL remains the source of truth for transactional data. Documents and events are synced to Elasticsearch via Kafka (see ADR-001) using Elasticsearch's Kafka connector.

Consequences:

Positive: Unified platform for full-text search, faceted navigation, aggregation-based analytics, and vector search. Kibana provides out-of-the-box dashboards without a separate BI tool. Elastic Inference Service removes the need for external embedding API accounts. Hybrid search (BM25 + semantic) is available without additional infrastructure.

Negative: Elasticsearch is eventually consistent relative to PostgreSQL — there will always be a small lag between a transactional write and its availability in search results. Teams must be aware of this when building features that require immediate search visibility after a write. Elasticsearch is also not suitable as a general-purpose relational store; joins and complex transactional logic must remain in PostgreSQL.

Alternatives considered:
1. Dedicated vector database (Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant) plus separate search engine — rejected because managing two separate systems for what is essentially the same retrieval problem adds unnecessary operational overhead.
2. PostgreSQL with pgvector — rejected because it lacks the hybrid search capabilities, horizontal scaling, and managed inference features we require.

Owner: Data Platform team. Review date: September 2025.

title: Deployment Process Guide
content:
This guide describes the standard process for deploying changes to production.

Overview: All deployments go through a CI/CD pipeline managed in GitHub Actions. Merging a pull request to the main branch triggers an automated build, test, and deploy sequence. Engineers do not deploy manually except during incident mitigation.

Environments: Changes move through three environments in sequence — development (auto-deployed on every commit to a feature branch), staging (auto-deployed on merge to main), and production (deployed from staging via a manual promotion step).

Promotion to production: To promote a staging build to production, open the GitHub Actions workflow for the relevant service and trigger the 'Deploy to Production' workflow manually. You will be prompted to confirm the staging build SHA you are promoting. Two-person approval is required for production deployments — the engineer initiating the deployment cannot be the same person who merged the PR.

Deployment windows: Production deployments are permitted Monday through Thursday between 10:00 and 16:00 in the primary on-call engineer's time zone. Deployments outside this window require explicit approval from the on-call engineer and must be recorded in the #deployments Slack channel.

Feature flags: All new features must be deployed behind a feature flag using our LaunchDarkly integration. This allows features to be enabled progressively (by percentage rollout or by user segment) and disabled instantly without a rollback if a problem is detected. A feature may only have its flag removed after it has been stable at 100% rollout for at least two weeks.

Monitoring a deployment: After a production deployment, the deploying engineer is responsible for monitoring error rates, latency, and pod health for at least 30 minutes. Use the 'Post-Deploy' Kibana dashboard for this. If any metric degrades by more than 10% relative to the pre-deploy baseline, initiate a rollback immediately (see runbook-k8s-rollback) and open an incident.

Hotfixes: A hotfix is a change that must bypass the normal staging promotion process due to a live production issue. Hotfixes require approval from both the on-call engineer and an engineering manager. The hotfix branch must still pass all CI checks before it can be deployed. After the hotfix, a retroactive PR must be opened against main to merge the fix into the normal development flow.

title: IT Laptop Setup Guide
content:
This guide walks you through setting up your company-issued laptop on your first day.

Step 1 — Power on and connect to Wi-Fi. Connect to the office Wi-Fi network 'Corp-Secure' using the credentials on the card in your onboarding pack. If you are setting up remotely, use your personal network temporarily — you will install the VPN client in step 3.

Step 2 — Sign in with your company account. Your IT-provisioned email address and temporary password were sent to your personal email. Sign in to your laptop using these credentials and change your password immediately. Your password must be at least 16 characters and must not be a password you use elsewhere.

Step 3 — Install the VPN client. Go to the IT portal (it.company.com) and download the VPN client for your operating system. Install it and connect using your company credentials. Once connected, all traffic to internal systems is routed through the VPN automatically.

Step 4 — Install 1Password. Download 1Password from the IT portal. Your IT administrator will send you an invitation to join the company 1Password account. Accept the invitation and set up your account. All shared credentials are stored here — do not store credentials anywhere else.

Step 5 — Install required software. The following tools are required for all employees and are available from the IT portal: Slack (use the company workspace link from your welcome email), Zoom, and your browser of choice with the company SSO extension installed.

Step 6 — Enable full-disk encryption. Go to System Settings > Privacy & Security and confirm that FileVault (macOS) or BitLocker (Windows) is enabled. If it is not enabled, enable it now and save the recovery key to 1Password.

Step 7 — Configure MFA. Go to the IT portal and enroll in multi-factor authentication using an authenticator app (not SMS). MFA is required for all company systems and will be enforced at your next login.

If you encounter any issues during setup, contact IT support via the #it-support Slack channel or by emailing it-support@company.com.
